_meta.description: Maps functional requirements to design constraints. When an agent describes what an object needs to DO, these rules translate into what the design needs to BE.

agent_guidance: Convert user's weight requirement to Newtons (1 kg = 9.81 N). Apply safety factor of 3x.
Cross-section area = (load_N * safety_factor) / material_capacity_n_per_mm2.
ALWAYS derate by 40% for inter-layer bond strength. FDM parts are 60% as strong across layers.
Orient the part so the primary load direction is ACROSS layers (compression), not ALONG them (tension/shear).
Example: PETG bracket holding 5 kg at 100mm cantilever: min cross-section = (49N * 3) / 25 = 5.9 mm2. Round up to 8mm x 3mm minimum.
PETG is the best choice. Excellent layer adhesion = fewest micro-gaps = most watertight.
Vase mode (single continuous wall, no seam) is naturally watertight for simple geometries.
For non-vase-mode: 4 shells minimum. The overlap between shells seals micro-gaps.
If food contact is required, add food-safe epoxy coating to interior regardless of material.
ASA is the only material with genuine UV resistance. Everything else degrades in direct sunlight.
If ASA isn't available (requires enclosure), PETG is the next best — survives outdoors for 1-2 years before becoming brittle.
PLA will warp and become brittle within weeks in direct sunlight. NEVER recommend for outdoor use.
For rain exposure: design with drainage channels and avoid flat horizontal surfaces that pool water.
Add mounting provisions — outdoor parts get wind-loaded. Through-bolts or concrete anchors depending on application.
PETG is the only common FDM material that is genuinely food-safe (same polymer family as water bottles).
PLA is 'food-safe' in pure form but FDM layer lines create bacterial harborage. Only use for single-use items (cookie cutters).
Coat interior surfaces with food-safe epoxy resin for any reusable food container regardless of material.
ABS, ASA, and Nylon are NOT food safe and should never be used for food contact.
Use a stainless steel nozzle — brass nozzles contain trace lead that can leach into food-contact surfaces.
4+ wall shells and 0.12-0.16mm layer height minimize gaps where bacteria can grow.
Match material heat deflection temp to the expected environment temp with 20C margin.
PLA deforms at 50-55C — rejects car interiors (80C+), near electronics (60C+), dishwashers (65C+).
PETG survives to ~65C. ABS/ASA to ~85C. PC to ~120C. Choose based on your temperature ceiling.
Annealing (controlled oven heating after printing) can raise heat deflection by 10-20C for PLA and PETG.
For electronics enclosures: calculate worst-case internal temperature (ambient + component heat). Add 20C safety margin.
TPU is the only FDM material with genuine flexibility. Shore hardness ranges from 85A (shoe sole) to 95A (car tire).
Verify printer has direct drive extruder. Bowden tube printers CANNOT reliably print TPU.
Print slow — 15-25 mm/s. TPU is elastic and buckles in the extruder at normal speeds.
Infill pattern and percentage control stiffness. 10% gyroid = very soft. 50% grid = quite stiff. Design accordingly.
For gaskets and seals: 0.2mm layer height, 100% infill, 3+ shells. This creates the most consistent seal surface.
Polycarbonate is king — bulletproof glass material. Nearly unbreakable in normal use.
Nylon is second — absorbs energy by deforming rather than shattering. Best for tools and repeated abuse.
ABS is third — the LEGO material. Good impact resistance, easier to print than PC or Nylon.
PLA SHATTERS on impact. It's rigid and brittle. Never use for protective cases or parts that get dropped.
Layer bond is the weak point for impact. 3+ shells and 40%+ infill. Solid infill near impact zones if possible.
Round all edges. Sharp corners are stress concentrators that initiate cracks on impact.
PLA has the best dimensional accuracy of any FDM material — lowest shrinkage, lowest warping.
Print at 0.12mm layer height for best dimensional accuracy. 0.2mm is fine for non-critical dimensions.
Add 0.2mm clearance on sliding fits, 0.1mm on snug fits. These are STARTING POINTS — printer-specific.
ALWAYS design a tolerance test piece first for any precision assembly. Print it, measure it, adjust your model.
Nylon absorbs moisture and swells. TPU is elastic and unpredictable. Neither works for precision.
Orient critical mating surfaces flat on the bed plate where possible — first layer is the most dimensionally accurate.
PLA produces the best surface finish of any FDM material. Matte PLA is especially good.
0.12mm layer height for visible surfaces. 0.08mm if the user demands exceptional quality (doubles print time).
Enable ironing for top surfaces — makes them glass-smooth at the cost of ~10% extra print time.
Use tree supports instead of grid supports — they leave smaller marks and are easier to remove.
Post-processing options: sanding (400-800 grit), filler primer + paint, vapor smoothing (ABS with acetone).
Silk PLA filaments give a metallic/pearl finish straight off the printer with no post-processing.
